What it's for. Workflow Automation runs rule-based automations when key events happen — for example, notifying a role, assigning a user, tagging an opportunity, creating a task, or calling a webhook. These are simple "when this happens, do that" rules (no AI involved).

Who can use it. All roles see Workflows in the sidebar; creating and managing workflows depends on your account's permissions.

Step by step.

  1. Open it. Select Workflows in the sidebar.
  2. Create a workflow. Give it a Name, choose a Trigger — Opportunity stage change, Bid/No-Bid decision, Proposal status change, Section approved, Compliance scan complete, or Debrief submitted — and optionally add conditions (key/value pairs that must match the event; use * to match any value, or leave empty to run on every trigger).
  3. Add actions (at least one required). Choose one or more actions: Send notification (message + recipient role), Assign user (role or user ID), Set opportunity tag (tag name), Create task (title + due-in-days), or Send webhook (webhook URL).
  4. Save and enable. Save the workflow; use its toggle to enable or disable it.
  5. Test and review runs. Use the test option (you can supply sample event data) and open a workflow to see its run history (or "No runs yet").
  6. Edit or delete. Update or remove a workflow.

What success looks like. The workflow is listed with its trigger and run count, and runs appear in its history when the trigger fires.

If something goes wrong.

  • "Name is required." / "At least one action is required." Add a name and at least one action.
  • "Failed to save workflow." Try again.
  • No runs recorded. The trigger hasn't fired yet, or conditions didn't match the event.

Tips & things to watch for.

  • Leave conditions empty to run on every occurrence of the trigger; add conditions to target specific cases (for example, only when a proposal moves to "submitted").

Related sections. Workflows react to events from Pipeline, Proposals, and Compliance.

6. System

6.1 Admin

What it's for. The Admin Console is the organization's control center — managing users and roles, sessions, the audit log, billing and plan, webhooks, the AI provider, and CPQ pricing configuration. Several Guided Setup wizards (onboarding, AI provider, SSO/MFA, ProPricer) also write their settings here.

Who can use it. Admin only. The Admin item appears in the sidebar solely for Admins.

Step by step.

  1. Open it. Select Admin in the sidebar to open the Admin Console.
  2. Use the tabs:
    • Users & Roles — invite/add users, set roles, deactivate or reactivate accounts, and perform bulk role updates or deactivations.
    • Sessions — view active user sessions and terminate them.
    • Audit Log — review recorded system events.
    • Billing & Plan — see your plan and usage limits.
    • Webhooks — manage outbound webhook connections.
    • AI Provider — connect your AI provider (OpenAI, Azure OpenAI, Anthropic, PursuiteAI, or a custom OpenAI-compatible endpoint), test the connection, and manage API keys. This is what enables AI drafting, the RFP Shredder, the Compliance Matrix, the pipeline, and other AI features across Pursuite.
    • CPQ — configure burden rates and labor-rate (ProPricer) settings used by quotes.
  3. Reach the admin sub-pages. From Admin you also manage Compliance Rules and Custom Fields (organization-wide configuration).

What success looks like. Changes save with confirmations (for example, "AI provider configuration saved," "Roles updated"), and new users and settings take effect across the organization.

If something goes wrong.

  • "Connection test failed. Check your credentials." Re-enter the AI provider details and test again.
  • "Failed to update roles." / "Bulk role update failed." Try again.
  • The Admin item isn't in your sidebar. Your role isn't Admin.

Tips & things to watch for.

  • AI features fail until a provider is connected and a successful connectivity test passes — set this up early via the AI Provider tab or the AI Provider wizard.
  • A newly saved proposal template stays a draft until an Admin publishes it (see Templates).

Related sections. Many settings here are also reachable through Guided Setup; roles set here govern access throughout the app.

6.2 Help

What it's for. Help is the in-app guide. It explains how to use Pursuite end to end — a quick start, a step-by-step workflow guide, a roles-and-permissions reference, and a catalog of features.

Who can use it. All roles.

Step by step.

  1. Open it. Select Help in the sidebar.
  2. Browse the sections, including Quick Start, Workflow Guide, Roles & Permissions, and feature references. Expand a workflow step to see instructions, where to go, and common friction points.

What success looks like. You find the guidance you need for the task at hand.

Tips & things to watch for.

  • The Roles & Permissions section is a quick way to confirm what your role can and can't do before you go looking for a missing button.

Related sections. Help points back to every area of the app described in this guide.

7. Guided Setup

What it's for. Guided Setup is a launcher of step-by-step wizards that walk you through common setup and onboarding tasks. It sits at the bottom of the sidebar (the ✨ Guided Setup button) and is also reachable inside the proposal workspace for the kickoff wizard.

Who can use it. Everyone sees the ✨ Guided Setup button, but the list of available wizards is filtered to what your role is allowed to run. The wizards and who can run them:

  • Proposal Kickoff: RFP → Draft-Ready (Proposal Manager) — turns an uploaded RFP into a draft-ready proposal.
  • New-Tenant Onboarding (Org Admin) — first-time setup for your organization.
  • AI Provider (BYOK) Connection (Org Admin) — connect and test your AI provider.
  • ProPricer Pricing-Bridge Setup (Admin / Pricing Lead) — connect ProPricer for live labor rates.
  • Integrations & Webhooks Connection (Admin / Integrator) — connect a webhook, mailbox, or calendar.
  • Teaming Partner Onboarding (Proposal Manager) — set up a teaming workspace and partners.
  • SSO + MFA + Clearance Governance (Org Admin) — configure single sign-on, MFA, and personnel clearances.

Step by step.

  1. Open the picker. Click ✨ Guided Setup at the bottom of the sidebar. Each wizard shows its title, audience, and step count.
  2. Start a wizard. Click Start. For wizards that act on a specific item (Proposal Kickoff, Teaming Partner), you'll be asked to paste the relevant ID (for example, a Proposal ID or Teaming workspace ID) before it launches.
  3. Work through the steps. Each wizard guides you step by step; some steps require an action (such as saving credentials or passing a connectivity test) before you can continue. Progress is saved, so you can close a wizard and resume it later from the same launcher.
  4. Finish. Completing the final step applies the setup (for example, "Credentials saved," "Requirements confirmed," "Bridge connected").

What the AI assistant does here. The Proposal Kickoff wizard ties into the AI features documented under Proposals: it ingests your uploaded RFP (extracting requirements via the RFP Shredder) and can generate a compliance matrix, then leaves you ready to draft. Review and confirm the extracted requirements inside the proposal's RFP Shredder panel before the wizard advances. The AI Provider wizard is what connects the AI engine that powers drafting across the app.

What success looks like. The wizard reaches its final step and reports success; the related feature (AI drafting, a connected integration, a draft-ready proposal) is now available.

If something goes wrong.

  • "No wizards available for your role." Your role can't run any setup wizards — an Admin or Proposal Manager handles these.
  • A step won't advance. That step requires a successful action first — for example, the AI Provider wizard needs a passing connectivity test, and Proposal Kickoff needs you to confirm RFP requirements in the proposal's RFP Shredder panel.
  • A connectivity or health test fails. Re-check the URL and key you entered and run the test again.

Tips & things to watch for.

  • Resource-scoped wizards (Proposal Kickoff, Teaming) need an ID up front — have the proposal or workspace open so you can copy it.
  • The Proposal Kickoff wizard can be started from the proposal workspace's ✨ Kickoff wizard button as well as from the sidebar.

Related sections. The wizards configure features documented under Proposals, Integrations, and Admin.

8. Your profile

What it's for. The profile area (titled Settings) is where you manage your own account — your display details, password, notification preferences, digest emails — and find quick links to connected tools. It's reached from the user block at the very bottom of the sidebar.

Who can use it. All roles, for their own account.

Step by step.

  1. Open it. Click your name/avatar at the bottom of the sidebar.
  2. Edit your profile. Under Account Settings, update your Photo, Display name, Email address, Time zone, and Date format; your Role is shown but not editable here. Click Save changes.
  3. Change your password. Enter your Current password (if you have one), then a New password (minimum 8 characters) and confirm it. Save to update it.
  4. Set notification preferences. Choose which notifications you receive and how, and configure digest emails.
  5. Jump to connected tools. Use the quick links to Integrations, SAM Monitors, and (Proposal Manager+) Scheduled Reports.

What success looks like. "Profile updated." and "Password updated successfully." confirmations; your display name, time zone, and date format apply across the app.

If something goes wrong.

  • "Passwords don't match." Re-enter the new password and confirmation so they match.
  • Password too short. Use at least 8 characters.

Tips & things to watch for.

  • Your Time zone and Date format affect how deadlines, reminders, and dates appear throughout Pursuite, including digest emails.

Related sections. Connected tools link to Integrations and Scheduled Reports.

Appendix: Items needing verification

This appendix is for the guide's author, not for end users. It lists anything that could not be fully confirmed from the application during writing.

  • Per-action role gates inside some screens were inferred from the on-screen permission checks present in each area (for example, management actions generally require Proposal Manager or higher). A few screens — notably Integrations and Workflows — perform their permission checks on the server rather than in the page, so the exact role required to create or change connections and workflows should be confirmed against the server's permission rules before publishing.
  • Webhooks tab (Admin) and SAM.gov Monitors are referenced from several screens (Home quick access, profile tools, dashboard widgets) but were not documented as their own sections because they aren't top-level sidebar items. If they should appear in the guide, confirm their exact on-screen labels and flows.
  • Pricing Volume access control — the precise rule for who can grant/revoke pricing access (versus who can simply view a granted volume) should be confirmed.
  • Presentation and Pricing approvals — the exact roles that can approve a generated presentation or a pricing volume were described as "Executive Approver / approver" based on surrounding UI; confirm against the server rules.
  • Bid/No-Bid threshold value — the numeric score at which a bid/no-bid escalates to Executive Approvers is shown dynamically on screen; the guide describes it as "the bid threshold" rather than a fixed number.
